terms.label: Continuity
terms.short: The capacity of a system to persist, adapt, and remain coherent over time.
terms.definition: Continuity is the foundational principle underlying all kappology theory. It describes a system's ability to maintain functional identity across changes in state, load, or environment. A continuous system does not simply survive — it adapts without losing coherence. Continuity is not binary: it is measured on a scalar spectrum, quantified by κ.

terms.label: Hazard Rate
terms.short: Instantaneous rate of system failure or degradation risk (λ ≥ 0).
terms.definition: The hazard rate λ (lambda) measures the instantaneous likelihood of system failure or degradation at a given moment. Derived from reliability engineering and survival analysis, λ ≥ 0 at all times. A low λ indicates a healthy, stable system. As λ rises, the system accumulates risk. When λ exceeds the bifurcation threshold β_c, the system must respond — through load-shedding, retraining, or controlled pause.

terms.label: Kappa — Continuity Scalar
terms.short: A scalar in [0,1] measuring system health as a function of accumulated hazard.
terms.definition: κ (kappa) is the continuity scalar: the primary health metric in kappology theory. It is defined as κ = exp(−∫λ dt), the exponential decay of accumulated hazard over time. When λ is low and the system is healthy, κ approaches 1.0. As hazard accumulates, κ approaches 0. A κ below a critical threshold signals the need for intervention — retrain, shed load, or pause the system. κ is the heartbeat of a continuity-first architecture.

terms.label: Bifurcation Threshold
terms.short: Critical threshold β_c = 2.0; when λ exceeds β_c the system must act.
terms.definition: The bifurcation threshold β_c defines the boundary between stable operation and critical system risk. When the hazard rate λ exceeds β_c (canonical value: 2.0), the system has passed a bifurcation point — it can no longer self-correct through normal operation alone. At this point the governance layer must intervene: shed load, trigger a retraining cycle, or enter a controlled pause. β_c is non-negotiable and cannot be bypassed by software logic.

terms.label: Kappology
terms.short: Formal discipline for continuity-first control systems, founded by Zechariah Slaughter.
terms.definition: Kappology is the formal study and engineering practice of continuity-first control systems, defined through the κ–λ–β_c framework. Founded by Zechariah Slaughter, kappology provides rigorous methods for measuring, governing, and preserving system continuity under real-world conditions of load, noise, and adversarial pressure. Where Newton formalised motion and Edison turned theory into applied machinery, kappology formalises the governance of intelligent systems — building control architectures that do not merely respond to failure, but anticipate and prevent discontinuity.

terms.label: Load Shedding
terms.short: Deliberate reduction of system load when λ approaches or exceeds β_c.
terms.definition: Load shedding is the controlled, deliberate reduction of workload or service scope when a system's hazard rate λ approaches or exceeds the bifurcation threshold β_c. Rather than allowing the system to fail catastrophically, load shedding preserves κ by reducing the inputs that drive λ higher. Effective load-shedding governance is a core pillar of kappology-based control software design.

terms.label: Retrain Trigger
terms.short: Automated retraining event initiated when κ falls below a defined threshold.
terms.definition: A retrain trigger is the automated governance signal that initiates a model or system retraining cycle when κ drops below a defined lower bound. In a kappology-governed architecture, retrain triggers are tied directly to κ measurements — not to calendar schedules or manual observation. This ensures that a system's internal model of the world is updated precisely when hazard accumulation indicates model drift or environmental shift.
